title: Podman tutorial lacks rootless setup instructions
body:
**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

Docs

**Description**

A friend ran through the initial Podman tutorial, and discovered that things immediately didn't work - he was getting the usual `lchown` errors from missing `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` mappings. However, he had no idea what was going on, because there are no rootless troubleshooting instructions in the tutorial, despite it recommending all commands be run as root.

If we want to run the tutorial without root, we need to start with a section on configuring your user for rootless Podman (subuid/subgid mappings, etc).

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Go through Podman tutorial in a user without `/etc/subuid` and `/etc/subgid` configured

2. Get confusing `lchown` errors saying that I don't have enough UIDs available in my namespace

3. Be very confused because none of this is explained

comments.body: We may want to combine the rootless tutorial and normal tutorial if we're recommending that the tutorial be run without root
@ipbabble I think you said you were going to touch up the tutorial with this in mind?  Am I mis-remembering?
This issue had no activity for 30 days. In the absence of activity or the "do-not-close" label, the issue will be automatically closed within 7 days.
Is this still the case?
Pretty sure the answer is yes - we need a tutorial rewrite
Yes, still the case.  @ipbabble keeps meaning to  get to it, but keeps getting higher priority stuff to do.
Who wrote the original tutorial btw?
I thiiink @TomSweeneyRedHat  but I also recall @baude did some work?
@ipbabble, one of us is losing it.  I thought you wrote it.....
I just checked the history, it's official, I'm losing it.  Looks like I started that one off, but I think I plagarized (with your OK) a demo script that you'd cobbled together.

"Cobbled"? "Cobbled"?  I'll have you know I don't cobble my demos! There was painstaking research and testing involved! ;-) LOL

FYI I have started looking at this myself and hit errors on using $ podman images  on fedora 31.  
$ sudo podman images worked fine. I have subuid and subgid files. in /etc/ 

So we need to just say what needs to be done to get this working (it worked on fedora 30. I wonder if I need to rm /etc/subuid /etc/subgid ?

I am on fedora 31 and I can confirm that `podman ps `has permission issues. Although `sudo podman ps` works  . I am new to this podman stuff but it worked pretty effortless without any issues on fedora 30 . I took the pain to install fedora 31 and come to know only that there are already open issues permission for rootless podman . 

I was hoping to get crio and kubernetes getting to work along with podman but the tutorial on that also are virtually non existent . Currently thats another topic for discussion but I am left with no choice as of now since rootless podman is the first step I need to get it running so that I can proceed with kubernetes with crio tutorial . 

Guys please help . Should I downgrade to fedora 30 or are there any resolution to this ( Again I am pretty noob and new to podman stuff ) 

Please put your thoughts out . Waiting .

When updating to Fedora 31, did you check your `libpod.conf`? For me it helped to move it away, and then run podman again to recreate it.

Also check out https://podman.io/blogs/2019/10/29/podman-crun-f31.html if you haven't yet.

@defyjoy as @ankon said, it's most likely your libpod.conf.   From you user's account, first make a copy of .config/containers/libpod.conf to libpod.conf_orig or something memorable.  Then remove the the libpod.conf file and run `ps info`.  That will generate a libpod.conf file.  That should get you up and running.  Then if you want to, you can edit the new libpod.conf file with any changes that you'd made to your old one.

If that doesn't do it for you, please let us know.

I tried the above solution however it is giving me the error as below - 

```
[joy-fedora@localhost ~]$ podman ps
Error: could not get runtime: error creating runtime static files directory /var/home/joy-fedora/.local/share/containers/storage/libpod: mkdir /var/home/joy-fedora/.local/share/containers: permission denied

```

Not sure how to resolve this .

I'd try `setenforce 0` to see if it's an SELinux issue. If that resolves it, posting the AVC would help us get it resolved. If not, it's probably an ownership/permissions issue with a subdirectory of the static files path listed there.

A separate issue is probably warranted if you want more assistance, though - we've strayed pretty far from the original topic here.
@mheon, is this issue still valid?
With regards to the tutorial? Yes, definitely - we've made some small improvements but it's still questionable if the initial tutorial should use rootless given the configuration difficulty
@ipbabble @TomSweeneyRedHat Would one of you want to take a stab at updating/creating the rootless tutorial

Fair. I will look at it tonight.

William

Hi. Found this thread as I had the same error. The error was resolved with 
`podman system migrate` and after having completed the /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid entries as mentioned above as a prerequisite. Hope it helps. My system setup is CentOS 7 and I used the latest podman (1.8.0) from the podman.io instructions 
`# CentOS 7`
`cd /etc/yum.repos.d/`
`sudo wget https://download.opensuse.org/repositories/devel:kubic:libcontainers:stable/CentOS_7/devel:kubic:libcontainers:stable.repo`
`sudo yum -y install podman`
Ref: [https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/rootless-podman-nfs
](url)
@ashley-cui Could you fix this up?

To add another datapoint, as an experienced 1.x podman user, I just installed 2.0 on a new machine and managed to find the rootless instructions in the man page, so that wasn't so bad. However, when trying to run a container, I was greeted with the following cryptic error message:

```
$ docker run --rm -it alpine
Trying to pull docker.io/library/alpine...
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob df20fa9351a1 done  
Copying config a24bb40132 done  
Writing manifest to image destination
Storing signatures
  Error processing tar file(exit status 1): there might not be enough IDs available in the namespace (requested 0:42 for /etc/shadow): lchown /etc/shadow: invalid argument
```

It wasn't until I found this issue and the mention of the command `podman system migrate` that things started working. Does this command need to be added to the rootless setup instructions as well?

Sorry, I should've been clear, I am indeed using the podman docker alias. My system is very new though, I'm using `podman` 2.0.1 from the Arch User Repository, and it was definitely not working (rootless) until I ran migrate, for whatever reason :thinking:. Maybe it is a red herring and I did something else to get it working, but I don't think so.

It's usually caused by the user running Podman at least once, realizing that they didn't have subuid/subgid set up, and then modifying those files and running Podman again. The files on disk are correctly populated, but the Podman pause process was made with the old, bad mappings, so things still fail without a `system migrate` to correct it.

In an ideal world, we would detect that the files have changed and recreate the pause process, but that seems somewhat complicated. Adjusting documentation will address things for now.
That definitely seems consistent with my experience, glad to have it cleared up.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
@mheon Is this still an issue, was the documentation adjusted? @TomSweeneyRedHat PTAL
Still on the list
@TomSweeneyRedHat Reminder

Just saw this one on ArchLinux. It doesn't help the warning is a bit confusing:

```
Error processing tar file(exit status 1): there might not be enough IDs available in the namespace (requested 0:42 for /etc/shadow): lchown /etc/shadow: invalid argument
```

Maybe the language needs updated to mention subuid's in some way?

Opened a bug to arch about it:
https://bugs.archlinux.org/task/68029
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

This definitely is documented now. Also some of the /etc/subuid and /etc/subgid issues are better handled.  If people want to open new issues on documentation or handling lack of subuid entries, please open new issues.
